title: The Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches — China's Ancient Time System
summary: The Stem-Branch system is one of the oldest continuous time-keeping systems in human history, still used today in Chinese astrology and calendar.
content:
The Heavenly Stems (天干) and Earthly Branches (地支), collectively known as Ganzhi, form the backbone of the traditional Chinese calendar and time-keeping system.

【The Ten Heavenly Stems】
Jia(甲), Yi(乙), Bing(丙), Ding(丁), Wu(戊), Ji(己), Geng(庚), Xin(辛), Ren(壬), Gui(癸)
Each stem belongs to one of the Five Elements, in yin-yang pairs: Jia(Yang Wood)-Yi(Yin Wood), Bing(Yang Fire)-Ding(Yin Fire), etc.

【The Twelve Earthly Branches】
Zi(子), Chou(丑), Yin(寅), Mao(卯), Chen(辰), Si(巳), Wu(午), Wei(未), Shen(申), You(酉), Xu(戌), Hai(亥)
Each branch corresponds to an animal sign, a month, and a two-hour period of the day.

【The Sixty-Year Cycle】
The stems and branches combine sequentially to form sixty unique pairs: Jia-Zi, Yi-Chou, Bing-Yin... and so on through Gui-Hai. This cycle is called the Sixty-Year Cycle or Sexagenary Cycle.

【Modern Uses】
The Ganzhi system remains active in: Ba Zi (Four Pillars) astrology, where a person's birth year, month, day, and hour each have a stem-branch pair; traditional Chinese calendar; choosing auspicious dates; and acupuncture timing.

title: Detailed Explanation of the Sixty-Year Cycle Nayin
summary: The Sixty-Year Cycle Nayin pairs the sixty sets of Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches with different musical tone names, forming a precise tool for destiny analysis.
content:
The Sixty-Year Cycle Nayin is a very unique aspect of the Stem-Branch theory. It assigns different Nayin names to each of the sixty Stem-Branch combinations.

【Sixty-Year Cycle Nayin Table】
Jiazi Yichou — Metal in the Sea    Bingyin Dingmao — Fire in the Furnace
Wuchen Jisi — Wood in the Great Forest    Gengwu Xinwei — Earth on the Roadside
Renshen Guiyou — Metal of the Sword Blade    Jiaxu Yihai — Fire on the Mountain Top
Bingzi Dingchou — Water in the Ravine    Wuyin Jimao — Earth on the City Wall
Gengchen Xinsi — Wax Metal    Renwu Guiwei — Willow Wood
Jiashen Yiyou — Water in the Spring    Bingxu Dinghai — Earth on the Roof
Wuzi Jichou — Thunder Fire    Gengyin Xinmao — Pine and Cypress Wood
Renchen Guisi — Flowing Water    Jiawu Yiwei — Metal in the Sand
Bingshen Dingyou — Fire at the Mountain Foot    Wuxu Jihai — Flat Land Wood
Gengzi Xinchou — Earth on the Wall    Renyin Guimao — Gold Foil
Jiachen Yisi — Lamp Fire    Bingwu Dingwei — Heavenly River Water
Wushen Jiyou — Great Post Earth    Gengxu Xinhai — Hairpin Gold
Renzi Guichou — Mulberry Wood    Jiayin Yimao — Great Stream Water
Bingchen Dingsi — Earth in the Sand    Wuwu Jiwei — Fire in the Sky
Gengshen Xinyou — Pomegranate Wood    Renxu Guihai — Great Sea Water

Nayin is primarily used in destiny analysis to assist in judging the temperament of a fate and analyzing the relationships of generation and control among the Five Elements.
